title: Third Way
alt: 'The monospaced-typewriter-font story is a COMPLETE FABRICATION!  WAKE UP, SHEEPLE' 'It doesn't matter! Studies support single spaces!' 'Those results weren't statistically significant!' 'Fine, you win. I'm using double spaces right now!' 'Are not!  We can all hear your stupid whitespace.'
transcript:
[To the left a group with three Cueballs, a Ponytail and Megan at the front which face another group with two Cueballs, a Ponytail and a black haired ponytail at the front. Each group has a placard. A Cueball in the left group has a cutlass and a Cueball in the right group has a spear as they are angrily facing off against each other. Off to the far right side stands a lone Cueball also with a placard.]
Left placard: Two spaces after a period
Middle placard: One space after a period
Right placard: Line break after every sentence
explanation:
This comic refers to the debate occurring in the United States about the correct . While typewriter typists in the United States were traditionally taught to use two spaces between sentences, this is becoming less common and many sources now recommend having only one space, although this topic is still . Cueball is advocating a line break after every sentence, the mysterious "third way". This obviates the problem, as a period will always appear at the end of a line and the spacing after it becomes moot. A line break after every sentence is sometimes called "semantic linefeeds". This is particularly useful when plain text files based on a markup language (such as , , or ) are edited by multiple people using a where it helps to facilitate comparison of changes and avoid merge conflicts. In markup languages such as that used in wikimedia, e.g. this site, a single line feed in the source is rendered as a simple space in a continuing paragraph of text, while two linefeeds generate a paragraph break. This approach allows the source to be easily manipulated and versioned, while the rendered output still keeps the regular flow and justification abilities of running text. HTML and languages derived from it will generally render multiple consecutive whitespace characters as a single in-line space (and raw HTML linefeeds are treated as just such a whitespace, no matter how many consecutive ones there are), so pretty much every page on the Internet uses single spacing, whether the author wants to or not, unless they specifically insert various hardcoded whitespaces (such as the " " non-breaking space character) or use suitable markup to enforce paragraph breaks (whether or not the HTML itself includes newline characters). and use some of the HTML standards (like contracting multiple simple-spaces to the bare minimum), invoking the aforementioned exceptions by either directly passing HTML-style markup to the browser or converting to that from a similar house-style element, but BBCode normally renders each raw linefeed as a paragraph break (via block-level HTML), while wikimarkup will convey some linefeeds (e.g. enforced for ":"-prefixed lines, for indentation, and upon the first newline within any table-cell contents). All these markups also tend to provide a "preformatted"-capability (e.g. 
, [code][/code] and space-prefixed lines) that will aim to preserve every whitespace as provided, often also to be displayed by fixed-width font.

The title text uses single spaces between the back-and-forth quotations; but within each quotation, the quoted speaker's preferred spacing is used; when the single-spacing advocate claims to be using double spacing, this is indeed a lie.

However, realistically, it is implausible that one can hear whitespace.

Randall's mocking characterization in the title text of overzealous advocates using the phrase "WAKE UP, SHEEPLE" has appeared in previous comics: 496: Secretary: Part 3 and 1013: Wake Up Sheeple.

Note that this is not the first time Randall has proposed a controversial third way, and this debate is later referenced in 1989: IMHO.

Sentence spacing was previously mentioned in the title text of 1070: Words for Small Sets.
